original_abstract: Traditional atom transfer radical polymerization (ATRP) often faces limitations in specialized applications like biomaterials and microelectronics due to residual metal contamination. To address this, we present an all-organic, metal-free ATRP platform mediated by light and catalyzed by a phenothiazine-based photoredox catalyst. Using 10-phenylphenothiazine (PTH), we achieved controlled polymerization of various vinyl monomers, demonstrated by a linear increase in molecular weight with conversion and the production of polymers with narrow dispersities. The system exhibits high temporal control, allowing the reaction to be efficiently toggled "on" and "off" in response to light irradiation. Mechanistic studies and mass spectrometry confirm high chain-end fidelity, matching the performance of conventional metal-catalyzed systems. Furthermore, the reducing nature of the PTH catalyst enables the polymerization of monomers previously incompatible with other photoredox systems and facilitates the synthesis of well-defined block copolymers. This metal-free approach expands the scope of controlled radical polymerization, offering a versatile and sustainable synthetic route for high-purity functional materials.
humanized_abstract: The use of traditional atom transfer radical polymerization (ATRP) is frequently hampered in the development of biomaterials and in certain applications in microelectronics due to the limitations imposed by the presence of transition metals. We here demonstrate that the use of light as activation trigger and an organic catalyst in the form of a phenothiazine derivative allows for all-organic, metal-free ATRP. Studies using 10-phenylphenothiazine (PTH) as the activator/catalyst showed that the vinyl monomers could be polymerized in a controlled fashion (linear increase in molecular weight with conversion, low dispersity) and that the on/off switching capability of the reaction is very good. Furthermore, the low mass spectrometry data of crude polymers confirmed high chain end fidelity as in traditional transition metal catalyst-triggered ATRP. Using the PTH activator/catalyst, monomers that do not polymerize under the action of other photoredox catalysts, due to either insufficient activation, or due to the reduction step, which can degrade the polymerization process, could be polymerized and block copolymers with high structural regularity could be prepared.

Furthermore, the low mass spectrometry data of crude polymers confirmed high chain end fidelity as in traditional transition metal catalyst-triggered ATRP.
